entry.title: 2H 2025 First Release of SAP Best Practices for SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll for USA
entry.content:
Dear all,

I hope you are doing well.

I am excited to announce the first release of SAP Best Practices for SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP), based on SAP Human Capital Management for SAP S/4HANA, for the United States (US) via the Solution Builder.

This is especially exciting for those of you looking for an accelerated implementation for Employee Central Payroll for the US customers.

What does this package include? The package consists of four key building blocks:

  1. Gross-to-Net Payroll Core
  2. Benefit Processing Core
  3. Post-Payroll Activities
  4. Absence Processing Core

(The configurations and possible extensions, along with an explanation of logic, are presented in our workbook, and you can refer to it for more details.)

 

1.Gross to Net Payroll Core

The building block Gross to Net Payroll Core bundles blocks and related configurations to implement the replication of payroll specific master data from Employee Central Core to Employee Central Payroll that is needed to successfully run payroll for USA employees. The implementation content is aligned with the best practice configurations delivered for Employee Central Core.

Personnel Administration

This process covers the replication of employee master data created during hiring or updated as result of performing changes in the employee file, as well as the correctness check of that data.

Key Features

Business Benefits

  • Maintain Company details.
  • Maintain Enterprise and Personnel Structure
  • Create Work schedules, Pay Structure and Organizational Information
  • Maintain PTP integration settings for Personnel Administration
  • Capture all basic employee data in one single source of truth
  • Smart business process execution
  • Reduces errors in self-service and boosts adoption through innovations, such as built-in wizards, smart business rules, org charts, audit history, and flexible workflows

Payroll Administration

This process describes the payroll administration. The configuration needed for the successful payroll processing are maintained in this section.

Key Features

Business Benefits

  • Payroll Schema and rules corrections
  • Maintain payroll area and periods
  • Maintain Payroll Control Record
  • Execute Payroll Simulation Run
  • Release Payroll Area for Correction
  • Correct Payroll Relevant Master Data and / or Time Data
  • Trigger Employee Master Data Replication
  • Configure the payroll wage types
  • Overpayment recovery process
  • Maintain PTP integration setting for Payroll
  • Enables the payroll processing and generation of payroll results
  • Helps to have full traceability of an employee’s payroll processing

Garnishment

This process describes the garnishment processing. The configuration relevant for garnishment processing is maintained in this section.

Key Features

Business Benefits

  • Master data setup needed for Garnishment
  • Specific configuration for service charges and order types
  • Adjustments and garnishment processing
  • Enables the garnishment processing and generation of garnishment results on payroll processing

Tax

This process describes tax validation and generation. The configuration relevant for tax processing is maintained in this section.

Key Features

Business Benefits

  • Tax company assignment to organization
  • Specific configuration for tax models
  • Enables tax processing and tax deduction during payroll processing

Posting to Finance

This process describes the configuration needed for posting the payroll result to the finance system and complete the payment process.

Key Features

Business Benefits

  • Employee grouping and accounts creation
  • Wage Type configuration and posting period maintenance
  • Posting variant creations
  • Execute Financial Accounting Posting Simulation Run
  • Create Test Payment Data using Preliminary Program DME
  • Enables the posting of payroll result into the finance system
  • Create Payment Data using Preliminary Program DME

 

2.Benefits Processing Core

The building block Benefits Processing Core bundles system configurations that are needed for performing benefits processing for employees in USA. It also describes implementation content for benefits processing in payroll.

401K Plan

Key Features

Business Benefits

● Employee grouping and 401K plans configuration settings.

● Configure other plans for the employees.

● View Employee Contribution Information

● View Employer Contribution Information

● Enables the 401K plans processing for the employees

 

3.Post Payroll Activities

The building block Post Payroll Activities bundles system configuration needed for the successful posting of the payroll results post the live payroll processing.

Third-Party Remittance

This process describes the configuration needed for maintaining the third-party remittance settings needed for remittance processing.

Key Features

Business Benefits

  • Execute Third-Party Remittance Run
  • Execute Third-Party Remittance Posting Run
  • Automatic calculation of remittance.

 

4.Absence Processing Core

This process describes the replication of absence data. After the time-off request has been approved, the absence period of the employee is marked as certain in the Employee Central system, and the time data is replicated automatically to Employee Central Payroll, where it can be checked for correctness.

View in Employee Central the replication status for each employee to see if there are any failed jobs. Use the Display or Overview function to view for the employee the absence data as transferred from Employee Central to Employee Central Payroll.

Absence Administration

Key Features

Business Benefits

● Trigger Employee Time Off Replication

● Update Employee Absence Record in Employee Central Payroll

● Initiate / Receive Employee Time Off Replication Confirmation Message

● Monitor Employee Time Off Replication

● View Employee Absence Data

● Enables employees to register their Leave of Absence at the period for which they are absent

● Helps to have full traceability when an employee takes a leave of absence and returns from the leave

 

These best practices add significant value by accelerating your implementations, and I hope to see increased adoption from you (partners) in the coming days.

I wish you the best of luck with your implementations.

If you have any feedback, please leave a comment below.

 

Thanks & best regards,

Sreenivasan Ravi

entry.title: Jumpstart Your HR Transformation with SAP Signavio Value Accelerators
entry.content:
The  SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library (VAL) provides ready-to-use, SAP-aligned process content that helps organizations move faster and with less risk during transformation programs. For SAP SuccessFactors customers and partners, standard, well-structured process content is very valuable: it reduces ambiguity during fit-to-standard workshops, creates a single source of truth for process owners and implementers, and shortens the time from discovery to design and deployment. 

The value accelerator for SAP SuccessFactors can directly be downloaded from VAL. In addition to that, the SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL), offers the opportunity for everybody to explore the value accelerator without the prerequisite of an own workspace. Built in SAP Signavio, the SSPL is the foundation for process design and standardization in the SAP SuccessFactors domain.  

No matter whether viewed via VAL or SSPL, the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator for Process Design and Standardization for SAP SuccessFactors  delivers more than just flowcharts: it provides interactive process artifacts that expose roles, system capabilities, data elements, AI-driven activities, and recommended actions for key moments that matter in your HR transformation journey. 

AI at the Heart of Modern HR Processes 

A standout feature of this release is the comprehensive "Driven by AI" attribute, which highlights how artificial intelligence supports your HR processes across three distinct categories: 

  • Deep Learning AI: Transform talent development and employee experience with personalized recommendations, predictions, and deep insights 
  • Generative AI: Accelerate HR processes and tasks by quickly generating or summarizing text across the SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite 
  • Joule (with Navigational and Transactional use cases): Deliver a better employee experience while lowering personnel costs with a natural language processing AI copilot 

These AI capabilities are clearly marked throughout the process models, helping you identify opportunities to leverage intelligent automation in your HR operations. 

Further Key Benefits at a Glance 

  • Fast ramp-up for SAP SuccessFactors transformation programs: Jump into fit-to-standard workshops with structured, SAP-aligned models instead of starting from scratch 
  • Improved alignment between HR operations, people managers, and IT through a common process language and clearly defined handovers 
  • Process harmonization: The layered architecture and navigation maps make it easy to identify overlaps and inconsistencies across regions and business units 
  • Solution design and configuration: BPMN models include system handoffs and data artifacts that help architects identify integration needs and data mapping requirements 
  • And more: Training and adoption support, reusable, governance-ready assets, ... 

Who Should Use This Content? 

This value accelerator is designed for: 

  • HR transformation teams and business process owners 
  • SAP SuccessFactors implementation partners and consultants 
  • IT architects and solution designers 
  • Business transformation managers and process excellence teams 
  • Anyone involved in HR digitalization and standardization initiatives 

What’s Included 

As described by @Marijn in the blog “Gain a comprehensive understanding of the processes in the SAP SuccessFactors process library”, the content comes with many useful artifacts: 

End-to-End Process Views and Hierarchies 

Within the SAP Signavio Process Collaboration Hub, you can explore processes in two main ways: 

  • End-to-End Process View: Navigate the complete HR process landscape 
  • Process Hierarchy: Found under "Shared Documents" in the left-hand panel  

Three New End-to-End Journey Models 

Additionally, there are three new journey models now available in the "Process Journeys" folder: 

  1. Recruit to Onboard 
  2. Develop to Grow 
  3. Reward to Retain 

These journeys help you introduce the SAP SuccessFactors process framework in a clear, structured, and business-friendly way. Each journey provides a high-level overview of the process landscape and serves as a visual guide to the roles, applications, and subprocesses involved, perfect for establishing shared understanding across stakeholders and accelerating onboarding for teams unfamiliar with SAP Signavio or SAP SuccessFactors. 

Note: Journey Model content is only available in SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL), not in the content downloadable from SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library. 

Rich Process Attributes  

Each process and activity includes detailed metadata: 

At the Process Level: 

  • Short Description, Purpose, Prerequisites, and Success Criteria 
  • Linkage to Business and Solution Capabilities 
  • AI support indicators 

Simply select a process of interest by clicking the “+” symbol and open up the panel on the right side to get the information. 

 

At the Activity Level: 

  • Fuchsia desktop icon: Indicates the SAP SuccessFactors capability used 
  • Orange person icon: Marks a "Moment that Matter" 
  • Cyan cogwheel icon: Denotes support by SAP Business AI 

Overlays highlight these attributes, while details can be explored by clicking on an activity which opens up the panel on the right side. 

Process Roles: 
Each swim lane represents a responsible role. Selecting a swim lane reveals: 

  • Role Description 
  • Process Role Group (e.g., HR or Business) 
  • Process Role Category: 
    • Business: "Manager" or "Individual Contributor" 
    • HR: Based on Dave Ulrich’s 3-pillar model: "HR Consultancy," "HR Expertise," "HR Operations," plus "HR IT" 

Data Elements: 
Activities may be linked to input/output documents. When selecting a document, the side panel displays - in addition to the description: 

  • Data Type (SuccessFactors, 3rd party, or non-system data) 
  • Data Element (for SAP SuccessFactors data) 
  • Data Element Grouping (e.g., EC data grouped under Personal or Employment Information) 

 

Downloadable Process Summaries 

Based on customer feedback, we’ve added one-pager process summaries containing: 

  • Key metadata for each process 
  • Direct link to the respective process in the SAP Signavio Collaboration Hub 

How to Access the Content 

Getting started is simple: 

  • Explore in SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL): Visit the SAP SuccessFactors Process Library in Signavio to explore the content interactively without needing your own workspace 
  • Download from the Value Accelerator Library (VAL): Download the complete package directly from VAL for use in your own SAP Signavio workspace.  

  • Watch the demo: Check out  this video for a complete walkthrough on how to explore the content in SSPL and download it from VAL

 

For a first overview, we encourage you to explore the journeys in the SAP SuccessFactors process library and use them as a starting point when introducing processes to your stakeholders. Additionally, you can leverage the process summaries for quick reference and stakeholder communication. 

You can look into these resources should you require further information on how to work with the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library:  

  • L1 Deck with overview of SAP Signavio Value Accelerators and their benefits. 
  • User guide for VAL on SAP Help. 
  • For more information on the SAP SuccessFactors process library, please refer to the SAP SuccessFactors Community. 

A big thank you to everyone who contributed to this release! Special kudos to the authors of the SAP SuccessFactors process content for their detailed modeling and practical guidance. Share your feedback with your HCM Business Partner or send us an email to SSPL@sap.com  to help us refine and improve these resources to drive future enhancements 

Ready to Transform Your HR Processes? 

Explore the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator for process design and standardization for SAP SuccessFactors today and turn process complexity into competitive advantage!

entry.title: Deep Dive: SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland
entry.content:
Deep Dive: SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland

Die Ankündigung die Gehaltsabrechnung für den deutschen Öffentlichen Dienst (ÖD) in den Lösungsumfang der SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll aufzunehmen, hat eine sehr positive Resonanz bei ÖD Kunden und die DSAG hervorgerufen. Nun ist es höchste Zeit konkreter zu werden!

In diesem Blog möchten wir folgende Themen erläutern:

  1.             Was beinhaltet die Lösungsumfangserweiterung von SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP)
  2.             Ausblick für das Jahr 2026 und folgende

Was ist für ÖD Kunden eigentlich drin?

Hier empfehlen wir einen Blick auf die Länderspezifika für SF Employee Central Payroll - Öffentlicher Dienst Deutschland  zu werfen.

Zusammengefasst: der Lösungsumfang der SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP) enthält ab 01. Januar 2026 alle Standardfunktionalitäten für die Personalabrechnung Öffentlicher Dienst Deutschland (PY-DE-PS). Die Fakten:

  • SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll basiert auf dem SAP HCM for SAP S/4HANA Stack
  • Die bekannten ÖD spezifischen Infotypen wie zB. IT0051 VBL/ZVE-Daten,  IT0509 Höherwertige Tätigkeiten,  IT0320 Dienstwohnung  IT0271 Statistiken des öffentlichen Diensts etc. sind wie gewohnt verfügbar
  • Pro Lohnart die im IT0014 Wiederkehrende Be-/Abzüge, IT0015 Ergänzende Zahlung oder IT2010 Entgeltbelege verarbeitet wird, wird definiert ob sie führend in  SF Employee Central oder in SF Employee Central Payroll gepflegt wird
  • Tarife wie zB. TVöD Bund, TVöD VKA, TVöD Pflege, TV-L etc. und ebenso die Beamtenbesoldung werden wie gewohnt abgebildet
  • ÖD spezifische Funktionalitäten wie zB. die automatische Berechnung der Urlaubs- und Krankenlohnaufschläge für unständige Bezüge, die Krankengeldzuschussberechnung, die zeitversetzte Verarbeitung der unständigen Bezüge oder die Altersteilzeit sind selbstverständlich im Standard vorhanden
  • Deutschland und ÖD spezifische Folgeaktivitäten sind im selben Umfang wie in SAP HCM for SAP S/4HANA bzw. bisher in SAP ERP HCM vorhanden
  • Das PY Schema D100 für die Abrechnung ist Teil der Auslieferung
  • Kundeneigenes Schemen und Zyklen können nach SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll übernommen werden
  • Customizing Aktivitäten und kundeneigene ABAP Programmierung z.B. über BadIs oder Z-Programme stehen zur Verfügung. Siehe Hierzu empfehlen wir die Dokumentation im SAP Help Portal zum Thema Modifikation und Erweiterbarkeit von SF Employee Central Payroll. 

 

Rahmenbedingungen für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland

Die wichtigste Botschaft: Der Scope der SF Employee Central Payroll  unterscheidet sich hinsichtlich der Personalabrechnung für den Öffentlichen Dienst Deutschland inklusive Folgeaktivitäten, Auswertungen und Statistiken nicht von SAP S/4HANA. 

Natürlich gilt der allgemeine Umfang der Lösung bzw. die Rahmenbedingungen gemäß der Produktbeschreibung und der Funktionsumfangsbeschreibung für SF Employee Central Payroll auch für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland. SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll ist der Teil der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite der für die Gehaltsabrechnung zuständig ist. Teile des HCM-Funktionsumfangs, die nicht direkt zur Gehaltsabrechnung gehören, sind wegen des starken Fokus auf Gehaltsabrechnung nicht im Lösungsumfang der EC Payroll verfügbar. Dazu gehören z.B. das Reisekostenmanagement, das Cross Application Time Sheet (CATS), die Learning Solution (LSO) oder eine Personaleinsatzplanung.

Dies vorausgeschickt möchten wir auf einige für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland wichtige Eigenheiten hinweisen. Zur besseren Lesbarkeit hier nur die zusätzlich für ÖD Kunden besonders relevanten Klarstellungen und Einschränkungen.

Kundenspezifische LösungVerwendung in Employee Central Payroll nicht erlaubt
Felder Dienstart/Unterdienstart in IT0001: Grundlegende Erweiterbarkeit: Zuordnung von Employee-Central-Feldern zu InfotypenVersorgungsadministration (VADM)
ZfA-Kommunikation über den SAP Standard hinausNachversicherungsadministration (NV)
 Organisationsmanagement Öffentliche Verwaltungen (PA-OM, PA-PM-OM)
 Stellenwirtschaft inklusive Obligoprozessor (PA-PM-CP) und Personal Budget Plan Management (PA-PM-BM)
 Reisekostenmanagement (FI-TV)
 Personaleinsatzplanung

Quelle: Länderspezifika für SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll - Öffentlicher Dienst Deutschland    Stand März 2026

Kunden aus dem Kern der öffentlichen Verwaltung z.B. aus Bund, Ländern und Kommunen, die VADM für die Versorgung Ihrer Beamten benötigen oder gesetzliche Anforderungen mit der Stellenwirtschaft befriedigen müssen, können dies nicht in der SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (Stand März 2026). Weiteres dazu im Kapitel Ausblick.

Empfehlung für den Umgang mit Basisbezügen für den ÖD

Aufgrund der Verknüpfungen zu verschiedenen anderen ÖD-spezifischen Infotypen wird bis auf weiteres empfohlen, Basisbezüge (Infotyp 0008) im öffentlichen Dienst direkt in Employee Central Payroll zu verwalten und nicht aus dem Compensation Portlet in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central zu replizieren.

Dementsprechend sollten Basisbezüge (Infotyp 0008) von der Point-to-Point Replikation im Customizing (Transaktion SPRO) ausgeschlossen werden, indem Sie die beschriebenen Schritte aus der ECP Dokumentation vornehmen.

 

Voraussetzungen für SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll

Kunden die bestehendes Customizing und / oder kundeneigenen Code aus einem bestehenden SAP HCM System übernehmen möchten, können dies tun solange Sie:

  1. Funktionsumfangsbeschreibung, Produktbeschreibung und dabei insbesondere Modifikation und Erweiterbarkeit von SAP SF Employee Central Payroll beachten
  2. Sicherstellen, dass sowohl Customizing als auch kundeneigener Code mit SAP S/4HANA kompatibel sind. Beachten sie dabei insbesondere die SAP Note 3091160 - SAP HCM für SAP S/4HANA – Allgemeiner Ansatz und Strategie, welche Aufschluss über HCM Simplification Items in S/4HANA gibt

Als Startpunkt zur Identifikation von Simplification Items bietet sich der SAP Readiness Check for SAP SuccessFactors HCM an. 

 

Ausblick für das Jahr 2026 und folgende

Zusammen mit unseren Kunden begeben wir uns auf die Reise dem Öffentliche Dienst in Deutschland mit der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite moderne HCM Prozesse digital in der Cloud zur Verfügung zu stellen, ohne dabei Abstriche in Sachen Sicherheit, Souveränität und Funktionsumfang zu machen. 

SAP ist bewusst, dass besonders Kunden aus dem Kern der öffentlichen Verwaltung auf Funktionalitäten wie Versorgungsadministration oder Stellenbewirtschaftung nicht verzichten können. Zusammen mit unseren Partnern und der DSAG arbeiten wir an diesen und weiteren Themen um letztlich mit allen Kunden aus dem Öffentlichen Dienst das Ziel zu erreichen.

Es gilt der übliche Disclaimer: Dies ist natürlich keine Zusage bestimmte Funktionalitäten zu einem bestimmten Zeitpunkt oder überhaupt zu liefern. Die Roadmap wird sobald konkret absehbar über die dafür vorgesehen Kanäle wie Roadmap Explorer oder What's New Viewer kommuniziert.

HR souverän und sicher - SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite in der SAP Sovereign Cloud

Die Sicherheitsmaßnahmen beim Betrieb von SAP Cloud Lösungen entsprechen den höchsten Standards für Cybersicherheit, Betrieb und Datenschutz. Dies gilt insbesondere für kritische Persönliche Mitarbeiter- und Organisationsdaten in der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite. Ausführlich informiert hier die Webseite SAP Trust Center - Sicherheit.

Zusätzliche SAP-Sovereign-Cloud-Angebote, sind eigenständige Angebote unseres Cloud-Portfolios, die auf die anspruchsvollsten Anforderungen in allen vier Dimensionen der digitalen Souveränität zugeschnitten sind: Daten, Betrieb, Recht und Technik.
Damit werden die strengeren regulatorischen und sicherheitstechnischen Anforderungen bestimmter Branchen, wie in teilen des Öffentlichen Dienstes, gefordert erfüllt.

Besonders relevant für unsere ÖD Kunden in Deutschland ist das in Deutschland gehostete Angebot SAP Cloud Infrastructure. Die Verfügbarkeit der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite inklusive Payroll ist dort bis Ende 2026 geplant.

Weitere Informationen finden Sie unter SAP Sovereign Cloud auf www.sap.com.

 

Fazit

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll basierend auf SAP S/4HANA  und tief integriert mit SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central  ist die moderne Payroll für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland. Dabei bietet sie aus Perspektive der Gehaltsabrechnung den vollen von SAP gewohnten Leistungsumfang in einem schlanken Kosteneffizienten Cloud Deployment. SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll vereint Effektivität, Effizienz und Sicherheit/Souveränität mit einer starken AI basierten Roadmap.

SAP arbeitet zusammen mit Partnern daran jedem Kunden den Umstieg auf die Full Cloud mit SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll zu ermöglichen.

Für Informationen zur SAP HCM Strategie, SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, Readiness Check for SAP SuccessFactors und zu weiteren Themen, empfehlen wir einen Besuch unserer HCM Move Seite.

entry.title: A small config change that saved my HR users a lot of headache — Fuzzy Search in SuccessFactors EC
entry.content:
Introduction

During one of my EC implementations in SAU project, I kept hearing the same complaint from HR users: they couldn't find employees through global search. A small issue on the surface, but it was slowing down daily work for the entire HR team. This post covers what was causing it and the one setting that fixed it.

What was happening

We were working with a client that had a large Arabic-speaking workforce and one thing you quickly notice in that context is that Arabic names transliterated into English don't have one fixed spelling. Mohammed, Muhammad, Mohamed all the same name, all spelled differently depending on who filled in the form.

HR users were complaining that they couldn't find employees through global search. My first reaction honestly was are they just not typing the name correctly? But after connected with a few of them and watching how they searched, I realized the problem wasn't them at all.

They were doing things like:

  • Typing Muhamed instead of Mohammed
  • Searching with a partial name like Fatim instead of Fatima
  • Small typos like Khaleed instead of Khalid 

and in all these cases the system just returned nothing, or completely irrelevant results. So they'd try again, rephrase, try again and wasting time on something that should take 5 seconds.

The fix — enabling Fuzzy Search

Fuzzy Search allows the system to return close matches even when the input is not exact. It handles spelling variations, partial names, and minor typos. Once I enabled it, the results changed immediately.

 

 

Before:

✗Muhamed → no results
✗Fatim → nothing useful
✗Khaleed → search fails

After:

✓Muhamed → suggests Mohamed
✓Fatim → returns Fatima
✓Khaleed → suggests Khalid
 

 

How to enable it

Simple Admin Center config — took me less than 2 minutes:

  1. Go to Admin Center
  2. Click on Tools
  3. Search for Company System and Logo Settings
  4. Enable "Fuzzy Search for People Search"

 Save Company System Settings

⏱Give it about 10 minutes to kick in

A few practical notes

One thing I noticed during testing Fuzzy Search works specifically in the Global Header People Search. It is not a system wide setting, so do not expect it to fix search issues in People Profile or the Employee Directory. Those areas work differently and have their own separate permissions and settings. If users are still complaining about search in those areas after you enable this, that is a separate investigation.

 

Conclusion

This experience was a good reminder that not every user complaint points to a training gap. Sometimes, the system simply is not configured to match real world usage.

In regions like Saudi Arabia, where Arabic name transliterations can vary significantly, enabling Fuzzy Search is not just a nice to have. It is practically essential for effective People Search.

If you are implementing SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central for a multilingual or Arabic speaking workforce and have not reviewed this setting yet, it is definitely worth checking. It is a quick, 2 minute configuration change that can greatly improve everyday usability.

And the next time a search issue is raised, take a moment to review the system configuration before assuming it is a user error. It might just save you from drawing the wrong conclusion.

entry.title: Saudi Arabia: GOSI Maternity Compensation – Why the “Obvious” Solution Doesn’t Work
entry.content:
Let’s Start with the Questions Everyone Is Asking

When the General Organization for Social Insurance (GOSI) introduced Maternity Compensation, two immediate questions come up in every discussion:

“If GOSI pays the employee… why don’t we just stop salary or reverse it?”
“And why is GOSI paying the employee directly, not the employer?”

Both questions are valid—and actually connected.

Why Does GOSI Pay the Employee Directly?

From a business perspective, many expect the payment to go to the employer first.
But the design from GOSI is intentional:

  • The benefit is meant to support the employee directly
  • It ensures the employee receives compensation without dependency on employer processing
  • It reduces the employer’s burden, but does not replace employer payroll processes automatically

Which creates the core challenge:

"Payroll still pays salary… while GOSI also pays the employee separately."

 

The Real Scenario Everyone Is Facing

Let’s look at what actually happens in companies:

  • An employee takes paid maternity leave, then after a few months receives compensation from GOSI.
Now the employer asks:

“How do I recover the salary I already paid?”

 

First Thought: “Let’s Just Reverse It”

Idea:

Replace paid maternity leave with unpaid leave (retroactively)

Why it sounds correct:
  • Aligns with the legal concept
  • Removes employer cost
  • Clean from a business perspective
Why it doesn’t really work:
  • SAP payroll will reopen past periods
  • Everything gets recalculated (even closed payroll)
  • Financial postings may change
  • Other wage types get impacted (not just maternity-related)

✔️So yes, it solves the business logic…
 ❎But breaks payroll stability

 

Second Thought: “Ok, Then Just Deduct It Later”

Idea:

Recover the amount through future payroll

Why we like it:
  • No retroactive impact
  • Keeps historical payroll unchanged
  • Easy to control
But here’s the catch:
  • We don’t know the exact GOSI amount
  • No clear rule on:
  • Basic vs Housing split

✔️So yes, it solves the technical issue…
❎But doesn’t solve the calculation ambiguity

 

Third Thought: “Can We Automate It Fully?”

Idea:

Build logic in payroll to calculate and distribute everything automatically

Why it’s attractive:
  • No manual work
  • Fully system-driven
  • Clean process
Why we didn’t go there (yet):
  • No confirmed formula from GOSI
-->To elaborate:

GOSI defines the compensation as 100% of the average insured salary over the last 12 months.
However, from a system perspective, this introduces multiple limitations:

  • The 12-month average may include periods where the employee worked for different employers
  • The current employer does not have visibility into salary or contribution data from previous companies
  • There is no integration/API providing this cross-employer historical data
  • Additionally, due to Personal Data Protection Law (PDPL) considerations, the employer does not have the authority to request or rely on the exact compensation amount received by the employee from GOSI
-->Which means:
Even if we automate the calculation in SAP, it would be based on incomplete or indirect data, and cannot be validated against the actual amount paid by GOSI
  • No integration/API to validate results
  • High risk of calculating the wrong amount

✔️So yes, it solves the automation need…
❎But risks compliance and accuracy

 

Fourth Thought: “What If We Handle It Correctly from the Start?”

Idea:

If the employee requests GOSI compensation during the same period, we directly record:

--> Unpaid maternity leave instead of paid leave

Why this is the best-case scenario:
  • No recovery required later
  • No retroactive changes
  • Payroll remains clean
  • Aligns with the GOSI concept
So… what’s still missing?
  • No visibility on actual GOSI compensation amount
  • No defined split between:
    • Basic Salary
    • Housing Allowance
  • Requires strong coordination:
    • HR
    • Payroll
    • Employee
  • Additionally, the employee may dispute the employer’s action based on Saudi Labor Law Article 151, especially if the expectation is to receive paid maternity leave from the employer

✔️ So yes, it solves the timing issue…
❎But introduces potential employee relations and legal interpretation risks, in addition to dependency on external clarity and internal discipline

 

So, What Did We Actually Do?

We stepped back and asked:

“What is the safest way to handle this—without breaking payroll or assuming unclear rules?”

--> That’s why we delivered this as a KBA (Knowledge-Based Approach)

 

Reference: SAP KBA

For detailed configuration steps and system behavior, please refer to the official SAP Knowledge Base Article: 🔗KBA 

This KBA provides:
  • Step-by-step implementation guidance
  • Detailed explanation of standard payroll behavior
  • Recommended approaches (IT0014, IT0045, IT2001)
  • Technical considerations for retroactivity and deductions

 

Why a KBA Instead of a Standard Solution?

Because:

  • GOSI doesn’t provide full calculation details
  • SAP doesn’t receive actual compensation values
  • Customers handle cases differently
  • Each scenario depends on timing and business decisions

--> A fixed solution would either:

  • Be too rigid
  • Or introduce incorrect calculations

--> A KBA provides:

  • Flexibility
  • Control
  • Safe implementation

 

Let’s Be Honest About the Options

Let’s look at each option realistically:

  • Unpaid Leave (Same Period)
    This is the cleanest approach from a process perspective, as it avoids any recovery later and keeps payroll aligned from the beginning. However, it depends heavily on early action and still suffers from unclear calculation rules.
  • Unpaid Leave (Retroactive)
    This can correct past payments and align with the legal concept, but it comes at a high cost—triggering retroactive payroll recalculations and impacting payroll stability.
  • Recurring Deduction (IT0014)
    A safe and practical method to recover the amount in future payroll periods. It is simple and controlled but relies on manual input since the exact calculation from GOSI is not available.
  • Loan (IT0045)
    Provides structured tracking and visibility of the remaining balance, making it suitable for financial control. However, it is more complex to manage and still depends on manual calculation.
  • Automation (PCR)
    This approach offers full automation and flexibility in distributing amounts across wage components. However, without a reliable calculation source from GOSI, it introduces a high risk of incorrect results.

 

The Hidden Problem: Net Pay & Arrears

Even with the best approach, another issue appears:

“What if the deduction is higher than the employee’s net salary?”

--> SAP will:

  • Deduct partially
  • Carry the remaining amount as arrears (claims)
  • Recover it in future payroll periods

--> Meaning:

  • Recovery becomes gradual
  • Not guaranteed in one cycle

 

The Big Missing Piece

Everything comes back to one point:

We still don’t have a clear answer from GOSI on:

  • Exact calculation method
  • Component distribution
  • Actual compensation value

 

So, What’s the Right Approach Today?

Not perfect but practical:

✅Handle correctly from the start when possible
✅Avoid retroactive payroll changes
✅Use forward recovery when needed
✅Keep logic flexible and controlled

 

Final Thought

This is one of those cases where:

The legal idea is simple…
But system reality is not.

Every solution we explored:

  • Solves one part of the problem
  • But not the full picture

--> That’s why flexibility not automation is the right approach for now

 

What We’re Waiting For

To move toward a standard solution, we need:

  • Clear calculation rules from GOSI
  • Integration or API availability
  • Standardized scenarios

 

Stay Updated

To stay informed about upcoming updates, enhancements, and discussions related to this topic, we recommend visiting the MENA SAP User Group space, where we will be sharing the latest developments and guidance.

entry.title: First Half 2026 Release: What’s New in SAP SuccessFactors HCM?
entry.content:
Today’s business environment is complex and uncertain, but one thing is clear—when your people and your business move in sync, you can respond to change with clarity and confidence.

With the SAP SuccessFactors HCM First Half 2026 Release, we are delivering more than 400 innovations across all solution areas, enabling you to strengthen connections across your people, data, and processes. Here’s a snapshot of what you can expect to see in this release:

Manage your workforce with agility

AI agents across core HR and payroll
We are introducing new agents designed to simplify tasks and enhance data accuracy:

  • Pending Workflow Agent – Surfaces workflow status and keeps tasks on track by nudging approvers or rerouting to avoid bottlenecks. It is designed to help cut time-to-resolution by up to 40%.
  • Employee Data Integration Agent – Detects data inconsistencies across core HR and payroll, proposes fixes, and applies approved changes to keep records accurate.
  • Payroll Alert Resolution Agent – Analyzes alerts, pinpoints likely root causes, and recommends prioritized fixes. It also enables natural‑language creation of validation rules.

Employee Data Integration Agent

 

Latest home page innovations
Web users can now view cards in the “Needs Attention” section as a full page as well as expand all custom cards into a single view, making it easier to scan, filter, and interact with many cards at once. Additionally, custom background images are now supported in the greeting section, allowing customers to adjust the look and feel to their preference.

Latest home page

 

Core HR workflow notification enhancements
Enhanced workflow notifications are now available, supporting faster, better decisions. Customers can use field mapping to define variables for these notifications to include pending (to-be-approved) data, providing approvers with the key context they need directly in email.

Enhanced demand definition in SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Scheduling
Organizations can now define labor needs by work center and shift, specifying headcount, required skills, and utilization. This provides a clear, consolidated view of planned resources while allowing planners to easily adjust and customize requirements.

Develop your people with purpose

AI agents across learning and talent management
We are introducing new agents designed to help optimize talent development processes:

  • Goal Creation Agent – Helps employees generate meaningful, actionable, and relevant goals aligned to their role and business priorities, delivering clarity in minutes.
  • Goal Progress Agent – Delivers continuous quality assurance by automatically spotting outdated, misaligned, or at‑risk goals and prompting timely refinements.
  • Learning Compliance Agent – Monitors training statuses and prompts employees to complete required learning on time, providing step-by-step guidance to keep users on track.
  • Succession Planning Agent – Provides proactive insights into succession plan health, surfaces risks, and delivers actionable insights to strengthen succession pipelines.

Succession Planning Agent

 

Enriched job architecture
The job profile builder now supports multilevel structures, allowing organizations to mirror real-world complexity. Skills and attributes cascade automatically across levels to streamline maintenance and provide clearer career pathways.

SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors integration
A new productized integration between SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors, SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, and SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding helps ensure data consistency and a seamless experience. Hiring teams benefit from SSO and a unified look and feel, simplifying interactions and enabling a faster time to hire.

SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors

 

Learning with intelligent Q&A
Users can now use Joule to surface immediate answers to questions about content in SAP SuccessFactors Learning. Drawing from courses, videos, documents, and other published materials, Joule delivers relevant resources and reference links to help learners find the information they need while reducing time spent navigating multiple sources.

Intelligent Q&A

 

Adapt to change with insight

New intelligent content
Customers can now access new intelligent content for People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud, helping support more informed people and business decisions. With pay transparency insights, organizations can strengthen compliance and support fair pay practices by analyzing compensation and identifying pay gaps. This capability provides the visibility needed to help meet the EU Pay Transparency Directive requirements. We are also introducing intelligent content for onboarding insights, time management insights, benefits insights, rewards and recognition insights, and compensation planning insights.

Pay Transparency Insights

 

Extend applications with ease

Work tech innovations
We are enhancing the SAP SuccessFactors app for Microsoft Teams to make it easier for employees to complete HR tasks directly within the flow of work. In this release, we are introducing calendar sync notifications for absence changes, helping ensure schedules stay up to date when an absence is updated or cancelled. Additionally, the Clock My Time quick action now supports geofencing so that employees can use Microsoft Teams on a supported mobile device to record time when physically present in an approved location. Finally, the Microsoft Teams app now supports onboarding notifications and HR ticket creation and tracking.

Geofencing in Microsoft Teams

 

Extensibility wizard
A new embedded extensibility wizard from SAP Business Technology Platform brings a guided, in-app experience to create custom extensions in SAP SuccessFactors HCM—whether adding a new data field, designing a UI card, or orchestrating a business process. This enables teams to accelerate delivery, align HR workflows to unique requirements, and streamline operations without compromising governance.

Extensibility Wizard

 

Learn more

These are just a few of the many innovations in the SAP SuccessFactors First Half 2026 Release. To learn more, check out the resources below:

  • Visit the SAP SuccessFactors Product Release & Road Map Information page for release documentation, product videos, and solution area blogs
  • Watch the 1H 2026 Release Highlights Overview Video, featuring Bianka Woelke, GVP Head of Application Product Management
  • Explore all innovations in the What's New Viewer

entry.title: Teşekkürler Doğan. TOGG zamanı: Yeni Bordro Vakti
entry.content:
Şimdi…

If Blog 1:

Fasten Your Seatbelt: Türkiye Payroll Is Ready for Takeoff

Önceki hikaye burada

 was about rebuilding the machine…

this one is about what happens when it takes off.

Because last time…

we talked about why.

Why the shift.

Why this yeni direction.

From Doğan… to TOGG.

demek öyle.

Şimdi… how?

Peki… nereden başlar?

Not payroll.

Never payroll.

Bordro değil.

It starts earlier.

With data.

With context.

With Employee Central.

Because before anything is calculated…

the system needs to know:

Who is this employee?
Where do they belong?
Which rules apply?

yani… payroll doesn't decide.

It reflects.

 

Once that is set…

the month already has a shape.

Inside Payroll Info.

IT0769.

Tax and Social Security Info.

That's how the system understands the employee from a legal view.

SGK group.
Tax group.
Related law number.
Exemptions.

yani… the legal identity behind payroll.

Gelir vergisi.
Damga vergisi.
SGK.
İstisna.

All in one place.

tek bir mekan.

 

And sometimes…

the employee does not start from zero.

New hire mid-year?
Org reassignment?

Continuity stays.

devam.

And now…

the employee starts working.

Which means…

time starts to matter.

Working days.
Weekends.
Absences.

Each one changes something.

SGK days.
Tax base.
Exemptions.

So before money even enters the picture…

the system needs to understand the month.

Not as one number…

parça parça.

Because if someone is unpaid for a few days…

those days shouldn't count.

SGK needs to see the real picture.

doğru.

That's where /T comes in.

A clean breakdown.

What was worked.
What was not.
What couldn't… sick leave, for example.

Once that is clear…

salary is not just calculated.

It is distributed.

You don't just see a total.

You see the story.

In the payslip.

güzel.

 

And sometimes…

some of that time…

is different.

Ar-Ge.

Which means…

we're no longer asking only "how much time".

But also…

"what type of time is this?"

Full-time Ar-Ge?

Already defined.

In Payroll Info.

IT0769.

Same place.

doğru.

But part-time…

starts from EC.

From the Time Sheet.

What actually happened.

Flows into payroll.

Through IT2010.

 

So now…

Time becomes logic.

Logic becomes split.

Will be explained.

biraz sabır 🙂

Ama… hayat gitmez

Things change.

Inside the same month.

An employee moves.

New workplace.
New legal entity.

Time is still there.

Logic is already there.

Including Ar-Ge.

But the context changes.

So the month… splits.

Not just by workplace.

But also by what happened.

Ar-Ge days.
Normal days.

yani… one split is not enough.

They layer.

 

Now you don't have one month.

You have parts.

Each with its own context.

And here…

we give it a name.

JUPER.

mühim olan bu.

And then…

money comes in.

Recurring payments.
Gross salary.
Benefits.

Yemek yardımı.

Özel sağlık sigortası.

Even family coverage…

with its own exemption logic.

But here is the important part…

customers choose the logic.

SGK days based?
Calendar days based?
Fixed 30-day distribution?

All possible.

işte şimdi güzel oluyor.

If structure splits…

amounts split too.

Different expectations.

Different payroll philosophies.

Same framework.

No custom chaos.

mühim olan bu.

But sometimes…

the law decides for you.

iş değişirse…
şirket değişirse…

the month is no longer theoretical.

It becomes calendar reality.

So even if another option was selected…

calendar days take over.

Harika!

Because workplace and company splits…

change the legal structure itself.

 

And here…

limits matter.

SGK taban.
SGK tavan.

What can be taken now.
What must wait.

And if there is excess SGK base?

It doesn't disappear.

It travels.

Across workplaces.

Across months.

ama kontrollü.

 

The smart part?

Transparency.

With one look in the payroll log…

you can see where the amount came from.

April?
March?

It is clear.

Not with multiple wage types.

One wage type.

Legal indicator.

çok akıllı.

So if we are in May…

and March excess is still there…

the system knows.

Bring it into May.

But don't push it forever.

Two-month rule.

Clear.

Controlled.

uyumlu.

And there are special cases too.

No working days?

Still, SGK may apply.

For example…

employee terminated.

Later receives a one-time bonus.

Zero workdays.

But SGK still needs to be calculated.

detay küçük… etki büyük.

And one more thing…

how the system calculates.

Gross-up is no longer separate.

It uses the same engine.

The same SGK.
The same tax functions.
The same exemption logic.

No duplicated logic.

No second brain.

gerek yok.

 

This whole structure was re-engineered…

so gross-up can reuse what already exists.

Same core.

Cleaner delivery.

Faster legal adaptation.

So when rules change…

you don't rewrite logic.

You reuse it.

işte bu.

Sonra… sonuç görünür

You see it.

 

Lines = splits.

doğru.

 

Nothing is lost.

Same exports.

Same control.

uyumlu.

No SGK days?

Still reported.

Zero stays zero.

güzel.

Ve çalışan ne görür?

All of this… shows up.

In the payslip.

Each split… one page.

 

Brüt.
Kesintiler.
Net.

Clean.

Structured.

güzel.

And if you don't want splits?

Diğer tarafta…

wage type reporter.

 

Rapor.

Toplam.

Audit.

Same rules.

Better structure.

işte bu.

entry.title: Max Success Plans Help Keep HR Transformation on Track
entry.content:
Every HR transformation starts with intent: there’s a clear ambition on the table to modernise HR, simplify processes, improve employee experience, get better insights, prepare for what comes next.  

The roadmap looks solid. The program is funded. The partners are aligned. Momentum is high.  And then reality kicks in: priorities shift, a regulatory requirement appears, a business unit needs to move faster than planned, a country goes live later (or earlier) than expected, a “temporary” workaround is introduced to keep things moving. Six months later, another decision builds on top of it. Another release arrives. Someone asks - is this the right time now to think about analytics? Or AI? Or extensions?  

None of these moments feel like failure. In fact, most of them feel entirely reasonable at the time. But taken together, they slowly pull the transformation off its original course. That’s what makes large‑scale HR transformation so difficult.  

It rarely breaks in one obvious place. It drifts: quietly, incrementally, and often invisibly, until the organisation finds itself with a solution that technically works, but is harder to scale, harder to govern, and harder to evolve than intended.  

Max Success Plan exists for exactly this stage of the journey. 

The Hidden Risk in Long‑Running HR Transformations 

Once the initial excitement of an HR transformation gives way to delivery, the nature of risk quietly changes. 

HR transformation is rarely a single project with a clear start and end. In most organisations, it unfolds as a multi‑phase journey spanning discovery, design, implementation, deployment, and long‑term operations - often over several years. During that time, teams are under constant pressure to adapt scope, accelerate delivery, make “temporary” compromises to keep momentum, introduce local variations, or respond to new regulatory and business demands.  

Taken individually, these decisions usually make sense. They help teams move forward, meet urgent needs, or unblock progress. The risk doesn’t come from any one choice, it comes from what happens when no one is actively safeguarding how those choices fit together over time. 

The impact is rarely dramatic or immediate. There is no single moment where the transformation is declared a failure. Instead, subtle issues begin to accumulate: architectural inconsistencies emerge, data models fragment, custom code and extensions multiply, user experience decisions stop scaling, and operational friction increases after go‑live. Each issue on its own feels manageable. Together, they quietly undermine the original transformation goals. 

By the time these problems become visible, addressing them is often expensive, disruptive, and politically difficult. The organisation is already live, already relying on the solution, and already tied into processes that are hard to unwind. 

This is the point where many HR leaders realise that momentum alone isn’t enough. What’s missing isn’t effort or expertise - it’s continuity. Someone needs to hold the transformation together across phases, decisions, and stakeholders, ensuring that short‑term progress doesn’t come at the cost of long‑term success. 

Transformation Needs Continuity, Not Just Momentum 

This is the point where many HR transformations begin to diverge. 

Most organisations are very good at generating momentum. They mobilise teams, align partners, fund programs, and push hard toward go‑live milestones. Considerable effort is invested in getting designs approved, configurations built, data migrated, and employees trained. But once go‑live is achieved, attention often shifts elsewhere: toward stabilisation, operations, and the next set of immediate business priorities. 

What tends to receive far less focus is what happens after the milestone is reached. 

In reality, the hardest part of HR transformation is not getting through individual phases, but maintaining continuity of intent as the organisation moves from design to build, from build to deploy, from deploy to run, and from run into continuous change. Each transition introduces new stakeholders, new constraints, and new pressures. Without a deliberate mechanism to carry decisions forward, the original transformation logic begins to fragment. 

This is where Max Success Plan becomes fundamentally different from other engagement models. 

It is not designed to accelerate individual tasks or optimise isolated activities. Its purpose is to protect the transformation itself, by providing sustained oversight, expert safeguarding, and architectural continuity across phases. Instead of treating each stage as a handover point, Max treats the transformation as a single, evolving journey, where decisions made early are continuously validated against long‑term goals. 

This shift - from momentum‑driven delivery to continuity‑driven transformation - is what allows complex HR programs to remain coherent, scalable, and governable over time. 

What Max Success Plan Actually Safeguards 

 

Max Success Plan builds on both the Foundational and Advanced Success Plans, but it adds something that long‑running HR transformations consistently lack: expert oversight of strategic decisions across the full lifecycle. 

This safeguarding spans the areas where drift and risk most often accumulate over time. It includes: 

  • HR architecture and hybrid cloud strategy, where early technical choices determine long‑term scalability 
  • It covers data migration and data volume management, ensuring quality and performance don’t erode as complexity grows 
  • It extends into UX and analytics strategy, so employee experience and insight capabilities remain coherent rather than fragmented 
  • It addresses integration and operational readiness, where small inconsistencies can create persistent friction 
  • And increasingly, it applies to AI, innovation, and extensibility decisions, where experimentation without guardrails can quickly outpace readiness.  
  • Just as importantly, it provides governance across partners and internal teams, helping align multiple contributors around a shared transformation intent. 

Max customers gain access to HCM Solution Architects, Coaches, and Experts‑on‑Demand who engage across the entire journey, from Discover through Run, not only at isolated checkpoints. Their role is not to take over delivery or replace implementation partners. Instead, they act as an independent layer of continuity, ensuring that the right decisions are made at the right time, with full visibility into downstream impact across systems, processes, and future phases. 

This is what allows complex HR transformations to evolve without losing coherence, and why Max is less about accelerating execution, and more about protecting the transformation as it unfolds. 

Keeping Transformations on Track Means Protecting Decisions 

Continuity becomes even harder to preserve as transformations progress, not because teams stop caring, but because the volume and frequency of decisions increase. Over time, the transformation demands constant judgement calls, often under pressure, often with incomplete information. This is where many HR programs begin to struggle, not due to lack of capability, but due to decision fatigue. 

One of the most common (and least visible) failure points in HR transformations is decision fatigue. 

As complexity grows, teams naturally start optimising locally. To keep delivery moving, a quick integration workaround is introduced. A UX shortcut is taken to meet a deadline. A data migration compromise is accepted to reduce immediate risk. An extension is built “just for now” because it seems easier than redesigning a process. Each of these decisions is rational in isolation, and often necessary at the time. 

The problem isn’t the decision itself, it’s the accumulation. Without transformation‑level safeguarding, these local optimisations stack up over months and years, quietly introducing technical debt and operational risk. What began as a coherent design starts to fragment. Integrations become harder to maintain. User experience loses consistency. Data quality and governance weaken. And fixing these issues later requires far more effort than preventing them in the first place. 

This is why Max Success Plan introduces structured checkpoints and expert reviews: not as bureaucracy, but as protection. Phase reviews, architecture points of view, feasibility checks, and value design governance exist to take the pressure off individual teams and ensure that short‑term progress does not undermine long‑term success. They create deliberate moments where decisions are reviewed in context, re‑validated against transformation goals, and adjusted before drift becomes embedded. 

In long‑running HR programs, this kind of decision support isn’t a slowdown. It’s what keeps the transformation intact as complexity increases. 

Why This Matters More as AI Enters HR 

AI accelerates everything, including risk. 

When organisations begin exploring AI‑enabled HR use cases, the margin for error narrows significantly. Decisions that once felt reversible suddenly are not. Data quality becomes foundational rather than “good enough.” Architectural choices shape what is possible for years to come. Governance expectations increase as AI touches sensitive people processes. Questions of trust, transparency, and ethics move from abstract discussions into day‑to‑day reality. 

In this context, the consequences of drift multiply. An architectural shortcut taken earlier can limit which AI capabilities can be adopted later. Fragmented data models undermine confidence in AI‑driven insights. Extensions built without a long‑term strategy become blockers rather than accelerators. And once AI is embedded into HR processes, undoing a poor decision is far more complex than avoiding it in the first place. 

This is where Max Success Plan plays a critical role. It helps organisations explore AI and innovation in a controlled, value‑driven way, ensuring that experimentation does not outpace readiness or compromise the foundation of the HR landscape. By combining expert oversight, architectural safeguarding, and structured governance, Max enables organisations to move forward with AI confidently, without sacrificing the integrity of the transformation they’ve already invested in. 

Transformation That Survives Contact with Reality 

Max Success Plan customers aren’t trying to simplify their environments by pretending complexity doesn’t exist. They are operating directly inside it: managing global HR processes, hybrid landscapes, long‑running programs, and continuous change as a normal state of business. The difference is intent. 

Instead of reacting to drift after it becomes visible, Max focuses on keeping the transformation on track while the business keeps moving. Decisions are reviewed in context, trade‑offs are made consciously, and the downstream impact of each choice is understood before it becomes embedded. 

Over time, this changes the character of the transformation. There are fewer painful course corrections, clearer governance across teams and partners, and HR platforms that remain scalable and coherent instead of slowly fragmenting. Most importantly, the solution continues to support strategic ambition long after the initial go‑live celebration has passed, when the real work of transformation actually begins. 

Is Max the Right Conversation to Have? 

At some point in a long‑running HR transformation, the questions change. 

It’s no longer just about whether the system works or whether teams are keeping up. The real question becomes whether the transformation can continue to scale, adapt, and evolve without losing coherence. That’s usually when organisations start recognising patterns in their own journey. 

If your HR transformation spans multiple years or phases, runs across SAP and non‑SAP systems, involves multiple partners and stakeholder groups, includes global payroll, data migration, or an emerging AI strategy, and must remain scalable and governable over time, then the challenges you’re facing are not tactical, they are structural. 

In that context, Max Success Plan isn’t about “more services” or additional delivery capacity. It’s about safeguarding the transformation you’re already investing in, by protecting architectural intent, decision quality, and operational readiness as complexity increases. 

For many customers, the right next step isn’t a technical deep dive or another milestone review. It’s a strategic conversation. A conversation with your Customer Success Manager or Account Executive to assess where your transformation is today, what risks are starting to emerge, and whether Max Success Plan is the right engagement model for where your HR landscape is heading next. 

Because when HR transformation becomes continuous, keeping it on track requires more than momentum. It requires intent, and the right structure around it.

entry.title: The HCM Ecosystem Is on a Roll and Sapphire Is Next
entry.content:
It has been a big few months for the SAP SuccessFactors partner community. From the energy on the floor in Las Vegas to over 1,050 partners gathering in Bangkok, the 2026 SAP Partner Summit series made one thing unmistakably clear: this ecosystem is firing on all cylinders.

Before we head into Sapphire, we wanted to take a moment to look back and share some highlights.

Bangkok and Las Vegas: What We Took Away

The theme across both summits was Together, We Elevate, and it showed. Customers are navigating real complexity: new technologies, evolving regulations, workforce expectations that are shifting faster than ever. And the partners making the biggest difference are the ones who show up not just with implementation skills, but with the kind of certified HCM expertise and regional depth that connects strategy to real business outcomes.

From the sessions in Las Vegas to the energy on the floor in Bangkok, the message was consistent. When partners and SAP SuccessFactors move together, customers win. It is what we heard from partners across every conversation at both events.

We also took time to recognize the partners raising the bar. Congratulations to all of the 2026 SAP Partner Award winners, including Deloitte for taking home HCM Excellence honors in Southeast Asia. The standard of work across this community continues to set the pace.

See It for Yourself

All of that energy, expertise, and partnership has been building for a long time. We captured some of it in this Partner Mashup video.

Join Us

With over 12,000 certified consultants across 100+ countries and 300+ certified partner apps, this is one of the most trusted partner ecosystems in HCM. And Sapphire is where we bring all of it together on the biggest stage of the year.

Sapphire — Virtual Join us online for the latest SAP SuccessFactors HCM innovations and hear how partners are helping customers realize the full value of their investment. Register now

SAP SuccessFactors Partner Summit — Amsterdam October 19–21 at RAI Amsterdam. The EMEA and MEE partner community comes together for three days of strategy, connection, and what is next for HCM. Learn more

entry.title: Strategic Change and SAP SuccessFactors: Why Senior HR Leaders Choose Max Success Plans
entry.content:
When we speak to Senior HR leaders they often say that they are being asked to do something fundamentally difficult: drive continuous organisational change while the ground beneath the organisation keeps shifting. They’re expected to modernise HR operations, harmonise workforce data, introduce AI responsibly, and enable faster workforce decisions - all without destabilising day‑to‑day operations or eroding trust.

The challenge isn’t recognising that transformation is ongoing. The harder question is how to structure the organisation around that reality so the HR platform can keep evolving: how to keep decisions coherent across regions and partners, how to maintain trust in data as the landscape changes, and how to adopt innovation without accumulating risk or complexity that slows the business down later.

For many senior HR leaders, this tension becomes most visible at the point where operational decisions begin to carry strategic weight. Choices about data models, process design, governance, or AI adoption no longer affect just HR efficiency; they shape how confidently the organisation can plan, respond, and grow. At that level, HR technology stops being a delivery concern and becomes a question of organisational capability.

That shift changes the role SAP SuccessFactors plays in the enterprise, and it changes what leaders need from the engagement model around it.

From HR Delivery to Strategic Enablement

At leadership level, HR technology decisions quickly move beyond implementation performance. The focus shifts to organisational capability: data quality leaders can trust, processes that scale across geographies, governance models that survive organisational change, and an operating model that can absorb innovation without disruption.

SAP SuccessFactors plays a central role in this shift, but senior HR leaders know that technology alone does not create capability. What matters just as much is how decisions are made, how trade‑offs are managed, and how intent is preserved as complexity grows.

Once HR technology becomes a strategic enabler rather than a delivery concern, leadership attention naturally shifts toward the areas where coherence is hardest to maintain over time. The visual below reflects three priorities that senior HR leaders consistently return to - not because they are new, but because they are the hardest to sustain as complexity, scale, and ambition increase.

What Max Success Plan provides is not a different set of goals, but a way to operationalise these priorities continuously: through governance, decision support, and expert safeguarding as the organisation evolves.

Harmonising Workforce Data and People Processes

For senior leaders, workforce data is no longer an operational output; it is a strategic asset. It underpins business planning, regulatory confidence, workforce decisions, and executive credibility. When data is inconsistent or fragmented, leaders feel the impact directly: through slower decisions, reduced confidence in analytics, and increased risk exposure.

In complex organisations, data fragmentation is rarely the result of poor intent. It emerges organically from legitimate local optimisation, phased rollouts, acquisitions, regional requirements, and evolving processes. Over time, however, these variations accumulate, and the organisation loses a shared understanding of what the data represents and how it should be used.

Max Success Plan addresses this challenge by safeguarding transformation design, architecture governance, and data and analytics adoption across the lifecycle. The focus is not on enforcing uniformity, but on ensuring that variation is intentional, explainable, and governable. This allows leaders to maintain a trusted data foundation, one they can rely on not just for reporting, but for strategic decisions that affect the entire workforce.

Enhancing HR Operations with AI - Responsibly

You already know this - AI has introduced a new level of urgency into HR leadership conversations. Senior HR leaders are expected to leverage AI to improve efficiency, insight, and employee experience, while simultaneously ensuring ethical use, transparency, compliance, and organisational readiness.

From a leadership perspective, the challenge is not access to AI capabilities. It is knowing when and how to deploy them responsibly at scale. Architectural dependencies, data readiness, governance models, and change maturity all determine whether AI becomes a strategic accelerator, or a source of unmanaged risk.

Max Success Plan supports leaders by combining architecture transformation, continuous innovation guidance, and business transformation management. This enables organisations to move beyond isolated experimentation and towards structured, value‑driven AI adoption. The result is not slower innovation, but innovation that enhances HR operations without undermining trust or stability.

Enabling Informed Workforce Decisions at Scale

As HR becomes more analytical, senior leaders are judged less on insight availability and more on decision quality. Having dashboards and data is no longer enough; leaders need confidence that insights are consistent, comparable, and actionable across the organisation.

This is particularly challenging in environments where systems, teams, and priorities continue to change. Without ongoing alignment, analytics frameworks drift, definitions diverge, and insights lose their strategic value.

Max Success Plan supports this priority by providing continuous evolution guidance and transformation safeguarding. This ensures that analytics adoption, decision frameworks, and governance remain aligned with the organisation’s operating model as it evolves. For leaders, this means they can act with confidence, even as complexity increases, knowing that the foundations supporting their decisions are being actively maintained.

Taken together, these priorities - trusted workforce data, responsible AI adoption, and decision‑quality at scale - point to something broader than operational excellence. They describe a leadership responsibility: ensuring that HR transformation remains aligned with enterprise ambition as complexity, pace, and expectation continue to increase.

This is where Max Success Plan moves beyond delivery support and becomes a distinct leadership instrument.

Why Max Is a Leadership Instrument, Not an IT Upgrade

At executive level, what distinguishes Max Success Plan is not the breadth of services it includes, but the nature of the questions it is designed to support.

Max exists to safeguard strategic intent across time, scale, and organisational change. It provides expert oversight at moments where decisions have long‑term implications: choices around architecture, data models, integration strategy, governance structures, extensibility, and AI adoption - often well before the consequences of those decisions become visible.

For senior HR leaders, this creates a different operating dynamic: instead of intervening reactively to correct drift or resolve misalignment after the fact, leaders gain confidence that the transformation is being continuously interpreted and aligned to strategic objectives as it evolves. The emphasis shifts from course‑correction to deliberate steering.

How Leaders Experience the Difference

Leaders who operate within a Max Success Plan engagement often describe a noticeable shift in where their time and attention are spent: there is less need to arbitrate architectural or governance debates once decisions are already embedded. Less effort is required to reconcile divergent interpretations of earlier design choices. Instead, conversations move earlier in the decision cycle and focus increasingly on future capability, organisational readiness, and opportunity.

This change is enabled by the presence of an independent, experienced layer of oversight whose sole responsibility is to safeguard the transformation as a whole, rather than individual initiatives, teams, or partners. That independence allows guidance to remain anchored to long‑term intent, even as priorities, stakeholders, and timelines change.

For leadership, this matters because it restores confidence in the transformation itself, not just in its delivery.

The Max Success Plan conversation typically begins when complexity is no longer something the organisation simply manages, but something that begins to shape leadership risk. This often occurs when SAP SuccessFactors underpins strategic workforce initiatives, when AI and analytics influence executive‑level decisions, or when the organisation’s ability to adapt becomes visibly linked to the coherence and credibility of its HR landscape.

At that point, the central question is no longer whether SAP SuccessFactors can support the organisation. It is whether the transformation around it is being actively guided with the same level of care and intent as the business strategy it enables. That is the role Max Success Plan is designed to play.

The next step is not a technical review, but a conversation with your Customer Success Manager or SAP Account Executive about whether your organisation would benefit from that level of partnership as it continues to evolve.

entry.title: Future-Dated Records on Terminated Employees: 1H 2026 Finally Closes a Long-Standing Gap.
entry.content:
If you've worked in Employee Central long enough, you know this pattern. Someone gets terminated, but a future-dated promotion, comp change, or position move is sitting in their record. Payroll picks it up. Replication chokes. Someone files a ticket Monday morning. Repeat.

Customers have been raising this for years. The 1H 2026 release finally addresses it through enhancement ECT-260432, driven by Customer Idea 249640.

What Actually Changes

When you terminate an employee, the system now automatically deletes any future-dated Job Information and Compensation Information records dated after the termination date. Related Position Management data follows the same rule. Time Off records are not covered, so keep your Termination End Handling process running for those.

The cleanup runs whether the termination flows through the UI (Latest or Legacy People Profile, Action Search, or Manage My Team), an import, or an OData API call. Every auto-deleted record lands in the audit logs, so you don't lose visibility.

There's also a smaller related improvement. If an admin tries to create a data change record on an already-terminated employee, the system now warns that the target user is inactive but still allows the save, because there are legitimate cases for it.

The Scenarios This Finally Puts to Rest

The mid-cycle resignation. A promotion approved last month, effective next month. The employee resigns in between. Until now, that future record sat there, payroll picked it up, replication choked, someone wrote a P1 ticket on a Monday morning.

The merit cycle ghost. April 1 comp updates pushed across the org. One employee resigns mid-March. Until now, the comp record stayed put, finance reports overstated forward salary spend, and someone wrote a manual reconciliation script.

The fiscal-year position move. A position change scheduled for the new fiscal year. Employee leaves before it kicks in. Until now, ghost position data confused the org chart and your S/4HANA replication target until the next data audit caught it.

If you've lived through any of these, you know none of them are exotic edge cases. They're Tuesday.

Two Things to Know Before You Turn It On

1. The default depends on the event reason type. For non-status-changing event reasons, the cleanup is automatically enabled and no action is required.

For status-changing event reasons, the "Delete Future-Dated Records After Terminate Action" toggle in Manage Organization, Pay and Job Structures → Event Reason defaults to No.

To extend cleanup to those, flip it to Yes per event reason.

Audit your event reason configuration before going live. That's the only way to make sure you actually catch every case relevant to your org. Note: this field can't be included in reports, so the audit has to be done in the configuration UI.

2. It does not apply to changes made through the History UI. If your admins use History UI to back-date or correct termination records, future-dated records will not be touched.

You'll still need a manual sweep for those flows. Worth flagging to your support team before the release lands.

Why This One Matters

This won't show up in any Joule launch video. There's no demo with sparkles. But for anyone who has spent a Monday morning chasing down a payroll replication failure caused by a comp record on someone who left two weeks ago, this is the kind of fix that quietly buys back weekends.

Audit your event reasons. Update your termination runbook. And give your operations team the news.

entry.title: Managing Complex HR Landscapes as a SAP SuccessFactors Customer: How Max Success Plan Supports
entry.content:
Managing Complex HR Landscapes as a SAP SuccessFactors Customer: How Max Success Plan Supports Multi‑Entity Organizations

SAP SuccessFactors may sit at the centre of your HR strategy, but it rarely operates alone: payroll, time and attendance, identity, analytics platforms, regional systems, and non‑SAP tools all orbit around it. Add multiple legal entities, regulatory requirements, and local operating models, and “keeping things consistent” becomes less about control and more about constant negotiation.

For most large organizations, the real challenge is no longer how to implement SAP SuccessFactors. It is how to keep a complex HR landscape coherent as it continues to evolve. This is where many transformations quietly begin to struggle.

When Complexity Turns into Fragmentation

Complexity on its own is not the problem. Large organizations are complex by design, and some degree of variation is unavoidable. The real risk emerges when complexity starts to fragment: when systems, processes, and decisions drift away from each other over time without a shared guiding structure.

Fragmentation often doesn’t show up as a failure, instead, it appears gradually: one region introduces a different process because it has unique constraints. Another adjusts an integration to resolve a local issue. A third adopts a release feature later, or not at all, because timing didn’t align. Each decision is reasonable in isolation. Together, they make the landscape harder to understand, harder to govern, and harder to scale.

Over time, organizations start asking familiar questions - why does the same employee action behave differently in different countries? Which data definition should analytics rely on? Who actually owns this integration now? Can we safely roll out a new capability globally without surprising half the organization?

Hopefully, you’ll agree with us that these are not configuration questions; they are governance questions.

Why Multi‑Entity HR Landscapes Are So Hard to Manage Over Time

In a multi‑entity environment, decisions about HR systems are rarely made by one team. Global HR may define standards, local HR may adapt them, IT may own integrations, partners may drive implementation choices. And over the years, people rotate, priorities change, and the original rationale behind decisions often fades or evolves to the point that it becomes unrecognisable as the time goes by.

It’s obvious, that what get lost here isn’t documentation, it’s context. And when context disappears, governance becomes reactive: teams spend more time explaining why something works the way it does than deciding what should come next. Change moves slower, not because people are resistant, but because no one has confidence in how many dependencies are attached to any given decision.

This is the reality many mature SAP SuccessFactors customers are operating in when they begin to consider Max Success Plan.

How Max Success Plan Supports a Multi‑Entity Reality

Max Success Plan does not attempt to simplify complex organizations into something they are not. Instead, it introduces structure and continuity around how complexity is designed, governed, and evolved.

Max customers work with HCM Solution Architects, Coaches, and Experts‑on‑Demand who engage across the full transformation lifecycle - from early discovery through long‑term operations. Their role is not to centralize control or replace local expertise. It is to maintain a coherent point of view across entities, systems, and partners as the HR landscape grows and changes.

This means:

  • architectural decisions are evaluated with long‑term scalability in mind, not just short‑term feasibility
  • Integration patterns are reviewed holistically, rather than region by region
  • Data migration and volume considerations are addressed as part of a broader data strategy, instead of being treated as one‑off projects.
  • UX and analytics decisions are aligned so insights remain meaningful across entities.
